title: Tadalafil vs. Avanafil
subtitle: PDE5 Inhibitor Kinetics, Selectivity & Market Dynamics — Verified, With a Skepticism Section
authors: User-provided clinical/market summary, fact-checked against FDA/EMA labeling and primary literature
sourceFile: user-provided content (chat); cross-checked against FDA Stendra/Cialis labels, EMA Spedra SmPC, and published PDE selectivity studies

items.title: Why is avanafil dosed only 15-30 minutes before sexual activity while on-demand tadalafil is dosed up to 2 hours prior?
items.content:
Avanafil is absorbed very rapidly, reaching a median peak plasma concentration (Tmax) of about 30-45 minutes in the fasted state.
Tadalafil is absorbed more slowly, with a Tmax of roughly 2 hours.
Dosing intervals before sexual activity are set to roughly track each drug's time to peak effect.
keyPoints: Avanafil fasted Tmax: ~30-45 minutes.
Tadalafil Tmax: ~2 hours.
On-demand dosing windows are built around each drug's absorption speed.
items.reference: FDA Stendra (avanafil) prescribing information, Clinical Pharmacology section: median Tmax 30-45 minutes fasted (accessdata.fda.gov/drugsatfda_docs/label/2018/202276s018lbl.pdf).

items.title: How does once-daily 5 mg tadalafil change the relationship between pill-taking and sexual activity compared to on-demand dosing?
items.content:
On-demand tadalafil (10-20 mg) is taken before an anticipated sexual encounter, like avanafil.
The 5 mg once-daily regimen instead maintains a continuous steady-state plasma concentration, so the act of taking the pill is fully decoupled from any specific sexual encounter.
keyPoints: On-demand tadalafil: dosed before an anticipated encounter.
Daily 5 mg tadalafil: steady-state dosing, no per-encounter timing needed.
items.reference: Cialis (tadalafil) prescribing information, Dosage and Administration section describing on-demand vs once-daily regimens.

items.title: What two indications does once-daily 5 mg tadalafil cover simultaneously?
items.content: Once-daily tadalafil 5 mg is FDA-approved both for erectile dysfunction and for the signs and symptoms of benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), and for men with both conditions concurrently.
keyPoints: Daily tadalafil 5 mg is approved for ED.
Daily tadalafil 5 mg is approved for BPH.
It is approved for concurrent ED + BPH.
items.reference: Cialis (tadalafil) prescribing information, Indications and Usage section (ED, BPH, and ED with BPH).

items.title: Why must neither tadalafil nor avanafil ever be co-administered with organic nitrates?
items.content:
Both drugs inhibit PDE5, the enzyme that breaks down cGMP generated by nitric oxide.
Organic nitrates independently raise nitric oxide/cGMP signaling; combining either PDE5 inhibitor with a nitrate produces synergistic vasodilation that can cause severe, refractory hypotension.
This is an absolute contraindication for the entire PDE5-inhibitor class, not specific to either drug.
keyPoints: PDE5 inhibitors + nitrates = synergistic vasodilation.
Can cause severe, refractory hypotension.
Absolute contraindication, class-wide.
items.reference: FDA labeling for both avanafil and tadalafil, Contraindications/Warnings sections regarding concomitant nitrate use.

items.title: Why do alpha-blockers require caution when co-prescribed with either PDE5 inhibitor?
items.content:
Alpha-blockers (commonly used for BPH or hypertension) independently cause vasodilation and reduce peripheral resistance.
Adding a PDE5 inhibitor can produce additive hypotension, particularly with dose initiation or titration, so both classes require careful dose titration when co-prescribed.
keyPoints: Alpha-blockers and PDE5 inhibitors both lower blood pressure.
Co-prescription requires cautious titration to avoid symptomatic hypotension.
items.reference: FDA labeling for avanafil and tadalafil, Drug Interactions sections regarding alpha-adrenergic blockers.

items.title: How does tadalafil's long half-life affect the duration of any adverse effect compared to avanafil?
items.content:
Tadalafil has a terminal elimination half-life of approximately 17.5 hours, so it takes roughly 3-4 days for the drug to largely clear the body — an adverse effect experienced after a dose can persist correspondingly longer.
Avanafil's commonly cited terminal half-life is much shorter (commonly reported as ~4-5 hours in U.S. data), so adverse effects generally resolve faster — though see the Skepticism section below: regulatory labels do not fully agree on avanafil's terminal half-life.
keyPoints: Tadalafil terminal half-life: ~17.5 hours (well-established, FDA label).
Tadalafil takes several days to substantially clear.
Avanafil's half-life is commonly cited as much shorter, but the exact figure is contested (see Skepticism section).
items.reference: Cialis (tadalafil) FDA prescribing information, Clinical Pharmacology section: terminal half-life ~17.5 hours.

items.title: Why is back pain/myalgia a recognized, drug-specific side effect of tadalafil within the PDE5-inhibitor class?
items.content:
PDE11 is expressed in skeletal muscle, testis, and prostate.
In cell-based selectivity studies, tadalafil's PDE5:PDE11 potency ratio is reported at roughly 40-fold — the lowest (least selective) of the three major PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil ~1000-fold, vardenafil ~9300-fold).
This comparatively weaker PDE11 selectivity is the leading hypothesis for why tadalafil, more than the other agents, is associated with back pain and myalgia, reported in roughly 6% of patients, typically with onset 12-24 hours post-dose and resolution within about 48 hours.
keyPoints: PDE11 is found in skeletal muscle, testis, and prostate.
Tadalafil's PDE5:PDE11 ratio (~40-fold) is the lowest of the three major agents.
Back pain/myalgia incidence with tadalafil: ~6%, onset 12-24h, resolves ~48h.
items.reference: Nature/International Journal of Impotence Research, tadalafil-associated back pain/myalgia incidence and etiology study (nature.com/articles/3901374); published PDE5/PDE11 selectivity comparison across tadalafil/vardenafil/sildenafil.

items.title: What is avanafil's verified selectivity advantage over PDE11 and PDE6 per its FDA label?
items.content:
Avanafil's FDA label states it is more than 100-fold more potent for PDE5 than for PDE6, and more than 10,000-fold more potent for PDE5 than for PDE11 (along with even larger margins over several other PDE isoenzymes).
This is the verified figure from the label — note that the user-provided source's specific "19,000:1" PDE11 figure is not what the label states (see Skepticism section).
keyPoints: FDA label: >100-fold selectivity for PDE5 over PDE6.
FDA label: >10,000-fold selectivity for PDE5 over PDE11.
These are the label's own stated floors, not precise point estimates.
items.reference: FDA Stendra (avanafil) prescribing information, Clinical Pharmacology/Mechanism of Action section: ">100-fold for PDE6... >10,000-fold for PDE1, PDE3, PDE9, and PDE11."

items.title: Why does avanafil's PDE6 selectivity translate into a lower risk of visual disturbance than first-generation sildenafil?
items.content:
PDE6 is the phosphodiesterase responsible for retinal phototransduction; inhibiting it is the classic mechanism behind sildenafil's well-known blue-tinted vision side effect.
Avanafil's much higher selectivity for PDE5 over PDE6 (>100-fold per its label) substantially reduces the likelihood of clinically apparent visual disturbance compared to sildenafil.
keyPoints: PDE6 inhibition underlies sildenafil's classic visual side effects.
Avanafil's >100-fold PDE5/PDE6 selectivity makes visual disturbance comparatively rare.
items.reference: FDA Stendra (avanafil) prescribing information, Clinical Pharmacology section on PDE6 selectivity; established mechanism of sildenafil-associated chromatopsia in PDE5-inhibitor pharmacology literature.

items.title: How does a high-fat meal actually affect avanafil's absorption, and is the source's "marginal delay" framing accurate?
items.content:
A high-fat meal delays avanafil's Tmax by a mean of about 1.12-1.25 hours (on top of a fasted Tmax of only 30-45 minutes) and reduces peak concentration (Cmax) by roughly 39%, while total systemic exposure (AUC) drops only minimally (about 3.8%).
So the source's claim that food does not change total exposure (AUC) is accurate — but calling the Tmax delay "marginal" understates it: more than an hour added onto a 30-45 minute fasted Tmax can meaningfully blunt the fast-onset advantage that is avanafil's main selling point. See the Skepticism section for more on this.
keyPoints: High-fat meal: Tmax delayed ~1.12-1.25 hours; Cmax down ~39%; AUC down only ~3.8%.
Total exposure (AUC) is largely preserved — the source is right about that.
But the absolute delay is large relative to avanafil's normal fast onset, undercutting its main clinical advantage if taken with a heavy meal.
items.reference: FDA Stendra (avanafil) prescribing information, Clinical Pharmacology section, food-effect data.

items.title: What rationale supports prescribing once-daily tadalafil beyond simple convenience?
items.content:
Once-daily tadalafil maintains continuous low-level PDE5 inhibition, which proponents describe as supporting ongoing endothelial function, in addition to its approved role in treating concurrent BPH symptoms.
This dual rationale (endothelial support + BPH treatment) is part of why daily dosing is favored for some patients over purely on-demand regimens.
keyPoints: Daily tadalafil maintains continuous PDE5 inhibition.
Treats concurrent BPH symptoms (FDA-approved use).
Marketed/discussed rationale includes ongoing endothelial support.
items.reference: Cialis (tadalafil) prescribing information, Indications and Usage and Clinical Studies sections (ED with BPH).

items.title: How should the "performance anxiety vs. scheduling anxiety" framing for choosing avanafil vs. daily tadalafil be understood?
items.content:
Describing avanafil as relieving "performance anxiety" (short waiting window) and daily tadalafil as relieving "scheduling anxiety" (no per-encounter timing) is a clinically intuitive conceptual framework that practitioners use to individualize drug choice to a patient's psychology and relationship context.
It is a reasonable shared-decision-making narrative, but it is not a labeled indication or an outcome that pivotal trials measured directly (those trials assessed IIEF score and intercourse success rate, not validated "anxiety loop" psychometrics) — treat it as interpretive clinical framing rather than as an evidence-based mechanism.
keyPoints: Useful conceptual framework for individualizing drug choice.
Not a trial-measured outcome or labeled indication.
Pivotal trials measured IIEF score and intercourse success rate, not anxiety constructs.
items.reference: Pivotal trial endpoints (IIEF, intercourse success rate) per FDA Stendra and Cialis Clinical Studies sections; the anxiety-loop framing itself is interpretive commentary, not a trial endpoint.

items.title: Why is avanafil's claimed ~19,000:1 PDE5:PDE11 selectivity ratio not actually supported by its own FDA label?
items.content:
The source states avanafil's PDE5:PDE11 selectivity ratio is approximately 19,000:1.
The FDA prescribing information instead states avanafil is "greater than 10,000-fold" more potent for PDE5 than PDE11 — a stated floor, not a precise point estimate, and it does not contain the figure 19,000.
Until a specific peer-reviewed source for "19,000" is identified, this number should be treated as unverified; the defensible, label-sourced claim is ">10,000-fold."
keyPoints: Source claims 19,000:1 — not found in the FDA label.
FDA label states only ">10,000-fold" selectivity for PDE5 over PDE11.
Use ">10,000-fold" as the verified figure until a primary source for 19,000 is found.
items.reference: FDA Stendra (avanafil) prescribing information, Clinical Pharmacology section: ">10,000-fold for PDE1, PDE3, PDE9, and PDE11" (accessdata.fda.gov/drugsatfda_docs/label/2018/202276s018lbl.pdf).

items.title: Why doesn't tadalafil's published PDE5:PDE11 ratio match the source's "5:1, mathematically guarantees back pain" claim?
items.content:
The source states tadalafil's PDE5:PDE11 ratio is about 5:1 and that this "mathematically guarantees" a 5-7% incidence of severe back pain/myalgia.
Published cell-based selectivity data instead report tadalafil's PDE5:PDE11 ratio at approximately 40-fold, not 5:1 — still the lowest of the three major agents, but roughly 8 times higher than the source's number.
Separately, the reported clinical incidence of back pain/myalgia with tadalafil (~6%) does land within the source's "5-7%" range, but the literature frames PDE11 inhibition as a leading hypothesis for this association, not a deterministic mathematical guarantee — individual variability, dose, and other unmeasured factors also contribute.
keyPoints: Source's "5:1" ratio is not what published selectivity studies report (~40-fold).
The ~6% incidence figure is roughly consistent with the source's "5-7%" claim.
"Mathematically guarantees" overstates a hypothesis as a deterministic certainty.
items.reference: Published PDE5/PDE11 selectivity comparison (tadalafil ~40-fold) and tadalafil back pain/myalgia incidence study, International Journal of Impotence Research (nature.com/articles/3901374).

items.title: Why do FDA and EMA labeling disagree on avanafil's terminal half-life?
items.content:
The source states avanafil's elimination half-life is approximately 5 hours, presented as a single clean figure.
U.S.-oriented summaries commonly cite a mean terminal half-life of about 4-5 hours (similar to sildenafil and vardenafil), but the EMA (European) product information for the same drug reports a terminal half-life of approximately 6-17 hours — a substantially wider and longer range.
This is a genuine, unresolved discrepancy between two major regulatory sources for the same pharmacokinetic parameter, not a settled single number.
keyPoints: Source presents "~5 hours" as a clean, settled figure.
EMA Spedra product information reports ~6-17 hours terminal half-life.
The two regulatory sources do not agree; treat "~5 hours" as the commonly cited but contested figure.
items.reference: EMA Spedra (avanafil) Summary of Product Characteristics, Pharmacokinetic properties section, vs. commonly cited U.S. terminal half-life figures.

items.title: Why is calling avanafil's high-fat-meal delay "marginal" potentially misleading for patient counseling?
items.content:
The source states a high-fat meal "can marginally delay" avanafil's peak concentration.
FDA data show the actual mean delay is 1.12-1.25 hours, added on top of a fasted Tmax of only 30-45 minutes — more than doubling or tripling the normal time to peak effect, and largely erasing the 15-30 minute fast-onset window that is avanafil's primary clinical selling point.
While total drug exposure (AUC) is indeed preserved, "marginal" is the wrong word for a delay of this relative magnitude when onset speed is the whole point of the drug.
keyPoints: Actual food-effect Tmax delay: 1.12-1.25 hours, not "marginal."
This is large relative to a 30-45 minute fasted Tmax.
AUC preservation does not mean onset speed is preserved — these are different claims.
items.reference: FDA Stendra (avanafil) prescribing information, Clinical Pharmacology section, food-effect data.

items.title: Why does the avanafil 'evergreening timed to patent cliffs' narrative not fully match the actual patent timeline?
items.content:
The source frames avanafil's 2012 launch as deliberately timed to coincide with sildenafil's and tadalafil's patent cliffs, calling it a classic evergreening strategy.
The actual timeline is more mixed: avanafil was FDA-approved in April 2012, within months of sildenafil's compound patent expiring (September 2012) — consistent with the narrative for sildenafil — but tadalafil's patent did not expire until 2015, and generic competition for both sildenafil and tadalafil did not substantially flood the market until 2017-2018, five-plus years after avanafil's launch. Avanafil itself remained patent-protected until 2022.
The synchrony with sildenafil's patent cliff is real; the synchrony with tadalafil's is overstated, and "evergreening" describes a plausible commercial motive rather than a documented, confirmed corporate intent.
keyPoints: Avanafil approved April 2012; sildenafil's patent expired September 2012 — close timing, consistent with the narrative.
Tadalafil's patent expired 2015; generic flooding of both drugs began 2017-2018 — years after avanafil launched.
Avanafil itself stayed patent-protected until 2022.
"Evergreening" is a plausible interpretation, not a confirmed documented intent.
items.reference: Avanafil FDA approval date (April 27, 2012) and patent-expiry timelines for sildenafil (Sept 2012), tadalafil (2015), and avanafil (2022) per public patent-tracking sources (drugpatentwatch.com, pharsight.greyb.com).

items.title: What needs independent confirmation about Cipla marketing avanafil in India under the brand name 'Avanair'?
items.content:
The source names Cipla's Indian avanafil brand as "Avanair."
Cipla does have products associated with both "Avana" and "Avanair" naming in the Indian avanafil market per pharmacy/distributor listings, but Indian generic drug branding is fragmented across many manufacturers and brand names, and distributor listings are not authoritative regulatory sources.
The specific brand-to-manufacturer mapping and market prominence (e.g., which name is Cipla's primary/flagship brand) should be confirmed against current Indian regulatory or company filings before being stated as a settled fact.
keyPoints: Both "Avana" and "Avanair" appear linked to Cipla avanafil products in distributor listings.
Distributor/pharmacy listings are not authoritative for brand ownership.
Treat the specific brand claim as needing confirmation against regulatory filings.
items.reference: Indian pharmaceutical distributor/pharmacy listings (e.g., drugssquare.com, indiamart.com) referencing Cipla avanafil brands "Avana" and "Avanair" — not a regulatory primary source.

items.question: A 58-year-old man with coronary artery disease on long-term isosorbide mononitrate for angina requests a PDE5 inhibitor for erectile dysfunction. Which of the following is correct?
options: Avanafil is safe to combine with nitrates because of its short half-life
Tadalafil is safe to combine with nitrates due to its slow Tmax
Both drugs are absolutely contraindicated with organic nitrates due to risk of severe, refractory hypotension
Only avanafil is contraindicated; tadalafil may be combined cautiously with nitrates
items.explanation: All PDE5 inhibitors, including both avanafil and tadalafil, are absolutely contraindicated with organic nitrates because of synergistic nitric oxide/cGMP-mediated vasodilation that can cause severe, refractory hypotension — this is class-wide, not specific to one drug's kinetics.
items.reference: FDA labeling for avanafil and tadalafil, Contraindications sections regarding concomitant nitrate use.

items.question: A patient wants the option to engage in sexual activity with minimal advance planning and asks which PDE5 inhibitor allows the shortest window between dosing and onset of effect. Based on Tmax data, which is correct?
options: Tadalafil, with a Tmax of ~2 hours
Avanafil, with a fasted Tmax of ~30-45 minutes
Both have identical Tmax
Neither drug's Tmax is relevant to dosing timing
items.explanation: Avanafil's fasted Tmax of about 30-45 minutes is markedly faster than tadalafil's ~2-hour Tmax, which is why avanafil is dosed only 15-30 minutes before anticipated activity.
items.reference: FDA Stendra (avanafil) prescribing information, Clinical Pharmacology section: median Tmax 30-45 minutes fasted.

items.question: A 62-year-old man has both erectile dysfunction and lower urinary tract symptoms from BPH. He prefers a single daily medication rather than per-encounter dosing. Which agent/regimen best fits this scenario?
options: On-demand avanafil 200 mg
On-demand tadalafil 20 mg
Once-daily tadalafil 5 mg
Once-daily avanafil 50 mg (no such regimen exists)
items.explanation: Once-daily tadalafil 5 mg is FDA-approved for both ED and BPH (and for concurrent ED with BPH), and maintains steady-state plasma levels that decouple dosing from any specific sexual encounter.
items.reference: Cialis (tadalafil) prescribing information, Indications and Usage section (ED, BPH, and ED with BPH).

items.question: A patient on tadalafil reports low back pain and diffuse myalgia beginning about 18 hours after his dose. What is the leading proposed mechanism?
options: Off-target inhibition of PDE6 in skeletal muscle
Off-target inhibition of PDE11, which is expressed in skeletal muscle, testis, and prostate
Direct nitrate-like vasodilation of paraspinal muscle vasculature
Accumulation of tadalafil in renal tubules causing referred pain
items.explanation: Tadalafil has the lowest PDE5:PDE11 selectivity of the major PDE5 inhibitors, and PDE11 is expressed in skeletal muscle, testis, and prostate — this off-target inhibition is the leading hypothesis for tadalafil-associated back pain/myalgia, typically beginning 12-24 hours post-dose.
items.reference: International Journal of Impotence Research, tadalafil-associated back pain/myalgia incidence and etiology study (nature.com/articles/3901374).

items.question: Among sildenafil, vardenafil, and tadalafil, which has the LOWEST (least favorable) PDE5:PDE11 selectivity ratio in published cell-based selectivity studies?
options: Sildenafil
Vardenafil
Tadalafil
All three are identical
items.explanation: Published selectivity data report PDE5:PDE11 ratios of approximately 1000-fold for sildenafil, 9300-fold for vardenafil, and only ~40-fold for tadalafil — making tadalafil the least selective of the three for PDE11, consistent with its higher association with myalgia/back pain.
items.reference: Published PDE5/PDE11 selectivity comparison across tadalafil, vardenafil, and sildenafil in PDE11-overexpressing cell studies.

items.question: Why is avanafil associated with a substantially lower risk of blue-tinted visual disturbance compared to first-generation sildenafil?
options: Avanafil does not cross the blood-retinal barrier
Avanafil has much higher selectivity for PDE5 over PDE6 (>100-fold per its label) than sildenafil
Avanafil is metabolized before reaching the retina
Avanafil is contraindicated in all patients with visual symptoms
items.explanation: PDE6 mediates retinal phototransduction; sildenafil's relatively lower PDE5/PDE6 selectivity is the classic basis for its visual side effects. Avanafil's FDA label states >100-fold selectivity for PDE5 over PDE6, substantially reducing this risk.
items.reference: FDA Stendra (avanafil) prescribing information, Clinical Pharmacology section on PDE6 selectivity.

items.question: A patient takes avanafil 30 minutes after a high-fat meal, expecting the usual fast onset. What is the most accurate counseling point?
options: Food has no effect on avanafil whatsoever
Total drug exposure (AUC) is preserved, but peak concentration is delayed by roughly an additional hour and reduced by about 39%, blunting the usual fast onset
A high-fat meal increases avanafil's half-life to 17 hours
Avanafil should never be taken within 24 hours of any meal
items.explanation: FDA data show a high-fat meal delays avanafil's Tmax by a mean of 1.12-1.25 hours and reduces Cmax by ~39%, even though total AUC is only minimally reduced (~3.8%) — so total exposure is preserved but the fast-onset advantage is significantly blunted.
items.reference: FDA Stendra (avanafil) prescribing information, Clinical Pharmacology section, food-effect data.

items.question: A patient develops a vasodilatory side effect (flushing, headache) after taking tadalafil. Compared to a similar side effect from avanafil, what is the key clinical implication of tadalafil's pharmacokinetics?
options: Tadalafil's effects resolve faster because of first-pass metabolism
Tadalafil's ~17.5-hour terminal half-life means the side effect can persist substantially longer than a similar effect from short-acting avanafil
There is no difference; both drugs have identical half-lives
Tadalafil's side effects are permanent and irreversible
items.explanation: Tadalafil's terminal half-life of approximately 17.5 hours means it takes several days to substantially clear, so adverse effects can persist longer than with the much shorter-acting avanafil.
items.reference: Cialis (tadalafil) FDA prescribing information, Clinical Pharmacology section: terminal half-life ~17.5 hours.

items.question: A patient on an alpha-blocker for BPH is started on a PDE5 inhibitor for ED. What precaution applies?
options: No precaution needed; alpha-blockers and PDE5 inhibitors have no interaction
Alpha-blockers and PDE5 inhibitors both lower blood pressure, so careful dose titration is needed to avoid additive hypotension
The alpha-blocker must be stopped permanently
This combination is only a concern with avanafil, not tadalafil
items.explanation: Both alpha-blockers and PDE5 inhibitors cause vasodilation; co-administration requires careful titration to avoid additive hypotension. This caution applies to both avanafil and tadalafil, not just one.
items.reference: FDA labeling for avanafil and tadalafil, Drug Interactions sections regarding alpha-adrenergic blockers.

items.question: A study guide states that avanafil's PDE5:PDE11 selectivity ratio is exactly 19,000:1, citing the FDA prescribing information. On checking the actual FDA label, what is the most accurate characterization of this claim?
options: The FDA label confirms exactly 19,000:1
The FDA label states only ">10,000-fold" selectivity for PDE5 over PDE11 — a floor, not the specific 19,000:1 figure cited
The FDA label does not discuss PDE11 selectivity at all
The true ratio is actually less than 1:1
items.explanation: The FDA label states avanafil's selectivity for PDE5 over PDE11 is ">10,000-fold," which is a stated minimum, not the precise 19,000:1 figure sometimes cited elsewhere without a traceable source.
items.reference: FDA Stendra (avanafil) prescribing information, Clinical Pharmacology section: ">10,000-fold for PDE1, PDE3, PDE9, and PDE11."

items.question: Which statement about the 'evergreening timed to patent cliffs' narrative surrounding avanafil's 2012 launch is best supported by the actual patent timeline?
options: Avanafil launched only after generic tadalafil had already dominated the market
Avanafil launched within months of sildenafil's patent expiry, but years before tadalafil's patent expired (2015) and well before generic competition flooded the market for both drugs (2017-2018)
Avanafil itself went generic before tadalafil did
The evergreening narrative is fully and precisely confirmed by FDA approval documents
items.explanation: Avanafil was approved in April 2012, close to sildenafil's September 2012 patent expiry, but tadalafil's patent did not expire until 2015 and generic flooding of both drugs did not occur until 2017-2018 — so the 'timed to the patent cliff' framing is partly but not fully supported by the actual timeline.
items.reference: Avanafil FDA approval date (April 27, 2012) and patent-expiry timelines for sildenafil (Sept 2012), tadalafil (2015), and avanafil (2022) per public patent-tracking sources.
